g.data-narration: Welcome to Binary Search Trees - the foundation of efficient searching and data organization in computer science.
BST enables O(log n) efficient searching - essential for databases, compilers, and building advanced trees like AVL and Red-Black.
g.text: Foundation for: AVL Trees, Red-Black Trees, Database Indexing

g.data-narration: A BST is a binary tree where all left subtree values are less than root, and all right subtree values are greater than root.
The BST property states: for any node with value X, all values in left subtree must be less than X, and all values in right subtree must be greater than X.
Search compares the key with root: if equal, found; if smaller, go left; if larger, go right. Recursively repeat until found or null.
Insert follows the search path: go left if key is smaller, right if larger, until reaching null position for new node.
Delete a leaf node: simply remove it - no restructuring needed since removing a leaf doesn't affect other nodes' positions.
g.text: No restructuring needed. Parent's pointer set to NULL.

g.data-narration: Delete node with one child: replace the node with its child - bypass the deleted node by linking parent directly to grandchild.
g.text: Bypass node: connect parent directly to child.

g.data-narration: Delete node with two children: find inorder successor (smallest in right subtree), copy its value, then delete the successor.
g.text: Find smallest in right subtree (9). Copy value, delete successor.

g.data-narration: Finding minimum: keep going left until null - leftmost node is minimum. Finding maximum: keep going right until null - rightmost is maximum.
Time complexity varies: O(log n) average for balanced trees, but O(n) worst case for skewed trees - same as a linked list.
